coredata.dc:description: Residual heavy metal ions in water pose a major hazard to ecology as well as human health, and Cu2+, as the most common heavy metal ion in water bodies, can cause a variety of diseases in human beings with prolonged exposure, therefore, a rational sensing platform is needed for the specific detection of Cu2+. In this work, based on the solvothermal method, we successfully prepared the composite UIO-66@FS by encapsulating the dye fluorescein sodium molecule (FS) with a metal–organic framework material (UIO-66). The composite material has bright fluorescence emission properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 62.03 %, and the composite material has been used to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for detecting the heavy metal Cu2+ in the aqueous environment. The fluorescence of UIO-66@FS can be greatly quenched by Cu2+, which is visible to the naked eye under UV lamp. The sensing platform is able to withstand environmental interference and has the advantages of high selectivity, excellent sensitivity, fast response, wide linear range (2.5 µM−500 µM), and low detection limit (0.246 µM) in the fluorescence quenching detection of Cu2+. In addition, the sensor has been used to detect Cu2+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries. Therefore, this sensing probe can be an excellent candidate for Cu2+ detection and has wonderful potential for real water sample detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Residual heavy metal ions in water pose a major hazard to ecology as well as human health, and Cu2+, as the most common heavy metal ion in water bodies, can cause a variety of diseases in human beings with prolonged exposure, therefore, a rational sensing platform is needed for the specific detection of Cu2+. In this work, based on the solvothermal method, we successfully prepared the composite UIO-66@FS by encapsulating the dye fluorescein sodium molecule (FS) with a metal–organic framework material (UIO-66). The composite material has bright fluorescence emission properties with a fluorescence quantum yield of 62.03 %, and the composite material has been used to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for detecting the heavy metal Cu2+ in the aqueous environment. The fluorescence of UIO-66@FS can be greatly quenched by Cu2+, which is visible to the naked eye under UV lamp. The sensing platform is able to withstand environmental interference and has the advantages of high selectivity, excellent sensitivity, fast response, wide linear range (2.5 µM−500 µM), and low detection limit (0.246 µM) in the fluorescence quenching detection of Cu2+. In addition, the sensor has been used to detect Cu2+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries. Therefore, this sensing probe can be an excellent candidate for Cu2+ detection and has wonderful potential for real water sample detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization and urbanization, much attention has been paid to the environmental pollution caused by heavy metal ions, which are often hidden in water bodies and pose a threat to human health. Heavy metal ions are of worldwide concern because they are difficult to be biodegraded and can be absorbed by living organisms [1]. Prolonged exposure to heavy metal ions or drinking water containing heavy metal ions in daily life may cause a series of unpleasant diseases, such as cancer, neurological disorders, decreased immunity, and kidney failure [2–5]. Carrying out tests for heavy metal ions is active and necessary. Among many heavy metal ions, Cu2+ is the most common heavy metal ion in human life. As an essential trace element in the human body, an appropriate amount of Cu2+ is crucial for controlling cellular metabolism [6]. However, long-term exposure to Cu2+ leads to its slow accumulation in the body, which can cause damage to multiple organs of the human body, and is prone to gastrointestinal diseases, alzheimer's disease, and spinal cord disease [7–10]. Currently, the detection methods for Cu2+ include ICP-mass spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), electrochemical method, flame atomic absorption spectrometry, etc [11–14]. Each of these methods has its own characteristics, but they are time-consuming and cost more money without getting real-time information feedback, so it is important to find alternative and convenient methods for the detection of heavy metal ions. Fortunately, fluorescence detection has been widely regarded by researchers because of its sensitivity, convenience, economy, and visualization for the detection of specific analytes [15]. Fluorescence detection for specific analytes requires the construction of a fluorescence sensing platform, which is the basis of fluorescence detection.

Metal-organic framework materials (MOFs) are considered to be wonderful candidates for building fluorescence sensing materials, and as an emerging coordination polymer material, it has a promising research prospect [16–19]. However, the more maturely studied MOF materials tend to have low quantum yields, leading to their limitation in the field of fluorescence sensing. Fortunately, MOF has a uniquely designable three-dimensional aperture, which can accommodate a variety of luminescent guests, based on which researchers can construct different types of composites, and the resulting composites tend to inherit the characteristics of the luminescent guests with high quantum yields and excellent fluorescence properties [20,21]. These luminescent guests include quantum dots, nanoparticles, and dye molecules, etc. Among them, organic luminescent dyes have high quantum yields and broad emission spectra, and their luminescence originates from the π-π conjugation of their color-generating groups, but inorganic dyes face the limitation of the aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ), and the disadvantage of easy agglomeration leads to the limitation of their applications [22]. Metal-organic framework materials can provide a excellent dispersion environment for dye molecules by encapsulating them, and the ordered pore structure of MOFs can separate the dye molecules and enhance the fluorescence by reducing the aggregation quenching, and the composites not only have photoluminescent properties, but the wonderful optical properties also promote their application in the field of fluorescence sensing, which makes them have a good potential for application [23–27]. Among the many metal–organic framework materials, the metal–organic framework materials of the UIO series are used in a large number of research applications, and is still emphasized by the majority of researchers in the field of fluorescence sensing, which is mainly attributed to the excellent pore structure and stability of the UIO series MOFs, which can be well adapted to a variety of detection environments, and has a wonderful potential for application [28–30].

Inspired by the above, the composite material (UIO-66@FS) based on metal–organic framework (UIO-66) encapsulated with sodium fluorescein (FS) dye was successfully constructed by solvothermal method. The composite material not only has wonderful stability, but also retains the excellent luminescent properties of sodium fluorescein, thus effectively improving the shortcomings of UIO-66 with very poor optical properties, and avoiding the shortcomings of sodium fluorescein which is easy to agglomerate and quenching. Subsequently, the composite material was applied to fluorescence detection of heavy metal Cu2+ in water, showing good selectivity and anti-interference performance. During the detection process, Cu2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of UIO-66@FS, and the process could be clearly seen under UV light. In addition, it has been shown to be useful for the detection of copper ions in real water samples.

Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) and terephthalic acid (H2BDC) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Science and Technology Co, and fluorescein sodium (FS) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. MnCl2·4H2O、NiCl2·6H2O、NaCl、PbCl2、AlCl3·6H2O、CuCl2·2H2O、BaCl2、LiCl, etc. were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. FeCl3·6H2O、MgCl2·6H2O、CdCl2·2.5H2O、AgNO3、HgCl2、NH4Cl, etc. were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. KCl、 Zn(NO3)2·6H2O、Co(NO3)2·6H2O、CaCl2, etc. were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide) and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Except for the actual water samples tested, the rest of the water samples were obtained from deionized water in our laboratory.

The synthesizing procedure of UIO-66 was as follows: ZrCl4 (0.025 g) was dissolved in a mixture of 10 mL of DMF and 2 mL of acetic acid, and stirred to dissolve, which was recorded as solution A. H2BDC (0.25 g) and FS (0.09 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF, and stirred to dissolve uniformly, which was recorded as solution B. Subsequently, solution A was mixed with solution B and then transferred to an autoclave reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner (Teflon), and was heated at 120 °C for 24 h. The reaction was cooled to room temperature at the end of the reaction, the reaction product was washed with DMF and ethanol solution and centrifuged in a centrifuge at 1000 r/min for 5 min until the washed supernatant was free of green fluorescence under UV light, then washed again with ethanol, and then the product was subsequently vacuum dried for 12 h to collect the yellowish solid powder to obtain UIO-66@FS. Fig. 1 demonstrates the sodium fluorescein molecular formula. Scheme 1 A shows the schematic of the synthesis route of UIO-66@FS solid powder. Scheme 1B shows the schematic of the structure of UIO-66 in situ encapsulated fluorescein sodium molecule.

The synthesis of UIO-66 is similar to the steps of UIO-66@FS, except that FS does not need to be added to the reaction raw material, and after solvent-heated synthesis, washing and drying, a white solid powder of UIO-66@FS can be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UIO-66@FS fluorescence quantum yield (QY)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is the ratio of the number of photons emitted to the photons absorbed after absorbing light energy of luminescent material, which reflects the luminescence efficiency of the luminescent material. There are two methods used to determine the quantum yield, a relative method [31,32] and an absolute method [33]. The measurement process of the relative method relies on a reference substance and has operational hassles that limit its application. In this study, the QY of UIO-66@FS was measured using Hamamatsu Absolute Quantum Yield Measurement System C9920-02G, and the spectra of the blank solvent and the UIO-66@FS solution are demonstrated in Fig. S1, first, the excitation-corrected intensities of the blank sample and UIO-66@FS were measured using the integrating sphere in Fig. S1A, and the number of absorbed excitation light photons, P1, was obtained by evaluating the area of the excitation peaks in the blank sample by subtracting the area of the excitation peaks of UIO-66@FS. Subsequently, the emission-corrected intensities of the blank sample and UIO-66@FS were measured by the integrating sphere in Fig. S1B, and the number of emitted photons, P2, was obtained by evaluating the emission peak area of UIO-66@FS minus the emission peak area of the blank sample. The absolute quantum yield was obtained from P2/P1, and the QY of UIO-66@FS was measured and analyzed to be 62.03 %. The assay is simpler, with less error in the testing process and better test stability.

Firstly, 50 mg of UIO-66@FS powder was dispersed into 50 mL of deionized aqueous solution and sonicated to make a homogeneous dispersion as the main body solution. Secondly, various metal ions solution (K+, Li+, Cd2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Na+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+) were prepared at a concentration of 10-2 M (except for Ag+, Zn2+, and CO2+, which are nitrates, the rest of the ions are chloride salts). For the specific assay, 500 μL of UIO-66@FS body solution was taken and 1500 μL of deionized water was added to prepare a fluorescence sensing solution with a total volume of 2 mL for use. Subsequently, 60 μL of aqueous solutions of different metal ions were added to the sensing solution, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded separately after shaking uniformly at a fluorescence excitation wavelength of 320 nm. After that, the anti-interference performance of the sensor was further explored by performing fluorescence titration experiments on Cu2+ aqueous solutions at different concentrations to ensure that the sensing material could realize the specific detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicality of UIO-66@FS, we chose laboratory tap water as well as Yellow River water from Lanzhou section in China as real samples for spiking recovery experiments. The real water samples were first filtered twice with 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the tiny insoluble particles in the experimental samples, and then a series of spiked Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations were prepared. Under the same conditions, their fluorescence data were tested for spiked recovery analysis.

Information about the material crystal structure was obtained by X-ray powder diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) tested on a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (AXS, Bruker, Germany). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) information was obtained by testing on a FTS-3000 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) information was tested and obtained on a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence spectral information was obtained on a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, USA). Sample morphology was obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, ULTRA plus, Germany) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi H-8100). The thermal stability of the samples was assessed by thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) (TG/DTA-6300, PerkineElmer) in N2 flow atmosphere from 25 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

First, the morphology and elemental composition of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS were analyzed using SEM, TEM, and EDS characterization techniques. The SEM images of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS are shown in Fig. 2 A and B, respectively, from which it can be seen that UIO-66 has a regular octahedral morphology and is well dispersed. After the introduction of FS, the edges of UIO-66 were passivated, making it easier to aggregate, but the host framework still maintained the octahedral configuration of UIO-66. Fig. 2C and D show the TEM images of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS, respectively. This further confirms that the freestanding UIO-66 has a regular octahedral morphology. In contrast, UIO-66@FS encapsulates a large number of dye molecules, which retains the structural integrity of UIO-66 from the edge structure. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2E, the analyzed EDS images clearly indicate that the elements C, O, Zr, and Na are uniformly distributed in UIO-66@FS, and the introduction of Na can only be attributed to sodium fluorescein (information on the elemental content composition of UIO-66@FS is displayed in Fig. S2). From the morphological as well as elemental analyses, sodium fluorescein was successfully introduced into UIO-66, but other means are needed to further analyze it.

The crystal structure information of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS was analyzed by XRD technique. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the crystal structure of UIO-66 is good, and the XRD pattern has typical characteristic peaks at diffraction angles of 7.35°, 8.43°, and 25.65°, representing (111), (200), and (600) crystallographic planar diffraction, respectively, and is in excellent agreement with the simulated pattern, indicating that the synthesis of UIO-66 is successful [34]. The XRD pattern of UIO-66@FS shows excellent agreement with UIO-66, which once again proves that the encapsulation of FS does not affect the crystal structure of UIO-66 significantly. The functional group information of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS was analyzed by FTIR technique. As shown in Fig. 3B, the absorption peak at 1638 cm−1 of the benzoquinone structure in the FS molecule is attributed to CO, whereas for UIO-66, the absorption peak at 550 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of Zr-O. After encapsulation of the FS molecule, the CO is red-shifted from 1638 cm−1 to 1643 cm−1, indicating that the FS molecule was successfully encapsulated into UIO-66 [35]. Notably, the position of the Zr-O peak of UIO-66 changed from 550 cm−1 to at 539 cm−1 after encapsulation of FS, indicating that the introduction of FS had little effect on the coordination of Zr4+ with H2BDC, and that the above slight change could be attributed to the weak interaction between FS and UIO-66 [36].

Nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments were used to explore the surface area as well as pore size distribution of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS. The experiments were carried out at 120 °C. as shown in Fig. 3C, the BET specific surface area of UIO-66 in the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm was 989.658 m2/g, while the specific surface area of UIO-66@FS was significantly reduced to 470.632 m2/g, which may be due to the large number of dye molecules loaded in the pore wall of UIO-66. After the introduction of FS, the crystallinity of some pore walls in UIO-66 may decrease or exhibit amorphous properties, resulting in a decrease in specific surface area [37,38]. In addition, from the pore size distribution plots in Fig. 3D, both UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS are typical microporous materials, and the number of micropores in UIO-66@FS is significantly reduced, but a new type of pore size distribution appears. This change can be attributed to the introduction of a large number of FS molecules into the micropores of UIO-66, and the distribution of these dye molecules is not in an irregular position, which has an effect on the pore size. Table S1 records detailed information about the specific surface area, average pore size, and pore volume size of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS. The above analysis further indicates that FS was successfully introduced into UIO-66 and the composites were successfully prepared [35,39].

The thermal stability of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS was explored using TGA and XRD analyses, and as shown in Fig. 4 A, UIO-66 exhibits a slow weight loss change compared to UIO-66@FS. The weight loss step before 120 °C can be attributed to the loss of trace water molecules adsorbed in the MOF cavities as well as ethanol molecules that were not completely removed during the washing and drying process. During the weight loss process at 200–500 °C, the weight loss of UIO-66 is relatively slow, which can be attributed to partial dissociation between metal ions and organic ligands, causing slow damage to the framework structure and resulting in a decrease in mass. However, the weight loss rate of UIO-66@FS is relatively fast, which may be attributed to the introduction of FS resulting in a decrease in the rigidity of the MOF framework compared to the original material, and a partial collapse of the framework occurs during the warming process, in addition, the mass loss may be related to the thermal decomposition of the FS molecules. After 500 °C, both the single material and the composite showed a significant weight loss process, which indicates that the framework structure of both collapsed significantly in this temperature range, leading to a serious mass loss. From the above analysis, even though UIO-66@FS possesses a faster thermal decomposition rate than UIO-66, its thermal stability is still maintained at a good level, which fully satisfies the temperature requirements needed for normal experimental environments. In addition, we also explored the structural stability of UIO-66@FS in high-temperature boiling water and analyzed the crystal structure using XRD tests. As shown in Fig. 4B, after adding UIO-66@FS powder into boiling water and continuously heating it for 24 h, we found that the XRD structures were in good agreement before and after heating, indicating good structural stability. The above analysis proves that UIO-66@FS has excellent thermal stability and can be used for subsequent experimental investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS
ce:section.ce:para: First, the solid-state fluorescence spectra of UIO-66, FS, and UIO-66@FS under 320 nm wavelength excitation were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, it can be seen that the sodium fluorescein dye has essentially no fluorescence emission properties in its solid state due to the strong aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ). The solid-state UIO-66 powder has a weak fluorescence emission near 400 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of the H2BDC ligand [40]. The solid-state UIO-66@FS powder exhibits dual fluorescence emission, the former attributed to the weak fluorescence emission of UIO-66 and the latter strong fluorescence emission at 505 nm attributed to the emission of sodium fluorescein [41], it was demonstrated that UIO-66 could be used as a carrier for dispersed FS to turn back on the fluorescence of FS that was quenched by the ACQ. The images of UIO-66, FS, and UIO-66@FS solid powders under daylight as well as UV light are shown in Fig. 5B, respectively. FS showed no fluorescence emission under daylight as well as UV light, which is consistent with the information of solid-state fluorescence spectra. Unlike daylight, UIO-66 shows extremely weak blue fluorescence emission under UV light, which also proves that UIO-66 cannot be used as a stand-alone fluorescence sensing platform for analyte detection. Same as the previous two is that UIO-66@FS powder also showed no fluorescence emission under daylight, but under UV excitation, UIO-66@FS powder showed bright yellow-green fluorescence emission, which indicates its potential for building a fluorescence sensing platform. In addition, after alkali decomposition of the composites, the mass percentage of FS in the composites was calculated by UV–visible spectrophotometry, and the loading percentage of FS was calculated to be 0.247 % (the detailed calculation procedure is shown in Supporting Information Fig. S3 and Table S2) [23]. As shown in Fig. 5C, the CIE chromaticity diagram also clearly demonstrates the color change of UIO-66 before and after encapsulation of FS. The above information also proves once again that the construction of UIO-66@FS composites was successful. It is worth noting that before exploring the fluorescence properties of UIO-66@FS in aqueous solution, it is necessary to verify whether the encapsulation of FS by UIO-66 is firm. As shown in Fig. 5D, after the UIO-66@FS powder was dispersed in deionized water and sonicated homogeneously and soaked for a long time, it can be seen that the composites settle to the bottom of the glass vials and the FS molecules are not released from the framework of UIO-66 as compared to pure water, even though the water solubility of FS is wonderful.

Thereafter, the fluorescence properties of the composites in water were explored and the mechanism of the fluorescence emission of the composites was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 6 A, similar to the fluorescence spectrum of the solid, the composites showed a weak and a strong fluorescence emission at 405 nm 505 nm, and because the two emission peaks overlapped, we chose the strong fluorescence emission wavelength attributed to FS for the subsequent sensing experiments of copper ions. It is noteworthy that it is important to understand the fluorescence emission mechanism of the composite material. The solid sodium fluorescein does not emit fluorescence, but after introducing it into UIO-66, the solid powder will produce fluorescence. What is the reason for this change? The possible reason is due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The FRET mechanism requires that the emission spectrum of the donor's emission group needs to overlap with the absorption spectrum of the receptor group to a certain extent. When excited by energy, the donor can transfer energy to the receptor, that is, when excited by the excitation wavelength of the donor, the fluorescence emission of the receptor can be observed [42–45]. To verify this, we tested the emission spectra of UIO-66 and the UV absorption spectra of FS in water, as shown in Fig. 6B. It was found that there is a significant spectral overlap between the two, indicating that the excitation energy of UIO-66 can be absorbed by FS, which in turn leads to its emission of fluorescence.

The feasibility of UIO-66@FS as a fluorescent probe needs to be further demonstrated. We dispersed the composite material powder in aqueous solutions under different testing conditions and further studied the fluorescence stability of the composite material under conventional and some extreme conditions. Firstly, a pure aqueous solution was used, as shown in Fig. 7 A and B. The aqueous solution of the composite material was stored for one week, and the fluorescence changes were observed every day, the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the composite material did not change significantly with time, indicating that the aqueous solution used for fluorescence sensing can be used for a long time. Subsequently, the change in fluorescence intensity of the composites in different pH was investigated, as shown in Fig. 7C and D. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the composites decreased under acidic as well as alkaline conditions, but the effect was not significant. Finally, the fluorescence stability of the composites in salt solutions of different concentrations was also explored, as shown in Fig. 7E and F. It can be seen that the composites have good salt resistance and the change in fluorescence intensity is not significant in aqueous solutions of 0.2–0.8 M NaCl. The above results indicate that the composite material has excellent resistance to external environmental interference and has the requirements for constructing a fluorescence sensing platform. The reason for analyzing its fluorescence stability is that the excellent protection ability of the UIO-66 host to the FS guest molecules enables it to have a good performance when facing extreme environments.

Due to the excellent fluorescence properties of the composites, we continued to explore their application in metal ion sensing. First, a selective test was conducted by adding different metal ions to the fluorescence sensing solution and recording their fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 8 A and B, different metal ions have different effects on the fluorescence intensity of the composites, with a small number of metal ions being able to slightly enhance the fluorescence of the composites, and most of the metal ions having a slight quenching effect on the fluorescence of the composites. However, it is worth noting that compared with other metal ions, Cu2+ can significantly quench the fluorescence of the composites, and the fluorescence intensity of the composites changed significantly before and after the addition of Cu2+, indicating that it can be used for the fluorescence detection of Cu2+. The fluorescence comparison images taken under UV light in Fig. S4 reflect the effects of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of UIO-66@FS, and the clear naked eye intuitively confirms the significant fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ on UIO-66@FS. Fig. 8C shows the fluorescence spectra of the composites before and after the detection of Cu2+, where the inset part is a photo before and after the detection of copper ions under UV lamp, from which it can be clearly seen that Cu2+ has a significant fluorescence quenching on the composites. The anti-interference performance is a key factor in evaluating the fluorescence sensing ability of the sensing materials, Therefore, the anti-interference performance of the composites was evaluated by sequentially adding the remaining interfering ions to the sensing solution with Cu2+ (interference ion concentration is 10 times higher than Cu2+ concentration), the fluorescence intensity changes of the sensing solutions were also tested to evaluate the anti-interference performance of the composites. As shown in Fig. 8D, indicate that the sensing solutions with Cu2+ were maintained at a roughly the same quench level and could not be interfered even with the addition of various remaining metal ions, proving that the composites have a good anti-interference ability and can be used for the specific detection of Cu2+. In Fig. S5, the photographs taken under the UV lamp reflect the effect of the presence of various interfering ions on the Cu2+ quenching of UIO-66@FS, clearly reflecting that the various metal ions did not have an obvious interfering effect, proving that the UIO-66@FS has good specificity for Cu2+ detection. To further enhance the persuasiveness, as a comparison, we further tested the effects of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of pure sodium fluorescein aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S6, the various metal ions did not cause significant changes to the luminescence intensity of sodium fluorescein, and only caused a slight decrease. It should be noted that Cu2+ did not cause a significant change in the FS fluorescence intensity, with only a slight quenching. The inset shows the fluorescence photos of FS aqueous solution alone and FS aqueous solution with Cu2+ added under UV lamp, which further proves that sodium fluorescein does not have the ability to detect Cu2+, thus making the detection of UIO-66@FS for Cu2+ more convincing.

Although the fluorescence quenching can be completed in a short time during the on-site detection under UV lamp, the actual fluorescence spectroscopy detection needs to meticulously evaluate the sensing time of the composite system because it is critical for the sensitivity enhancement, therefore, the time optimization was carried out before the fluorescence titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 9 A, after the addition of Cu2+ solution, the fluorescence intensity of the composites decreased rapidly at 30 s, and had a slight decreasing trend after 1 min until it remained stable, indicating that the composites possessed a fast response time. Subsequently, we performed fluorescence titration experiments to investigate the effect of different concentrations of Cu2+ aqueous solution on the fluorescence intensity of UIO-66@FS solution. As shown in Fig. 9B, the fluorescence intensity of UIO-66@FS at 505 nm gradually decreased with the gradual increase of the copper ion concentration (2.5–1000 µM) and reached the end point of the titration gradually. Fig. 9C and D reflect the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of the UIO-66@FS sensing solution and the change of copper ion concentration, from which it can be clearly seen that the change of the relative fluorescence intensity of the composite material is nonlinear with Cu2+ in the concentration interval of 2.5–1000 µM, but exhibits a good linear relationship in the concentration interval of 2.5–500 µM, proving that the composite material has the advantage of a wide linear range for Cu2+ detection. According to the Stern-Völmer equation: F0/F = Ksv[M] + 1, where F0/F is the fluorescence intensity before and after the detection of Cu2+ by UIO-66@FS, [M] stands for the concentration of Cu2+, and Ksv is the quenching constant, and the value of Ksv is calculated to be 2.17 × 10-3 M−1, with a linear correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.998, and the equation of the linear curve is y = 0.00217[M] + 1.053. The detection limit of the sensing system was further calculated based on the equation LOD = 3σ/K (S/N = 5), and the calculated LOD = 0.246 µM, which is much lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) standard that the concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water should not exceed 20 µM [46]. The fluorescence sensing platform based on UIO-66@FS has a excellent potential for detecting Cu2+ in real water samples because of its ability to effectively overcome the external interference compared to some other materials that have been reported, and has the advantage of a wide linear range with low detection limits. In Table 1 , a comparison of the performance parameters of UIO-66@FS with other reported materials for Cu2+ detection is given.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of UIO-66@FS for the detection of Cu2+ in tap water and Yellow River water (China-Lanzhou section) was further investigated. From the information reflected in Table 2 , it can be seen that the UIO-66@FS composite material showed recoveries of 97.75 %–101.30 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.17 %–2.74 % in the detection of Cu2+ in tap water. In the detection of Cu2+ in Yellow River water, the recoveries were 98.46 %–102.40 % with the RSD of 1.95 %–2.90 %. It shows that the sensor can be used for the detection of Cu2+ in real environmental water samples with satisfactory results.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cu2+ on UIO-66@FS composites was analyzed from several perspectives. First, the fluorescence lifetime of UIO-66@FS before and after the detection of Cu2+ was explored by the fluorescence lifetime technique. As a comparison, pure FS and UIO-66 were also used to detect Cu2+, and their fluorescence lifetimes were subsequently recorded and analyzed separately. As shown in Fig. 10 , the UIO-66 solution has a very weak intensity of its own fluorescence, and its fluorescence lifetime before and after the detection of Cu2+ did not change, which was 0.78 ns, indicating that it does not participate in the dynamic quenching process of Cu2+, and the fluorescence spectra, as shown in Fig. S7, confirmed the above view. The fluorescence lifetime before and after the detection of Cu2+ by pure sodium fluorescein dye changed slightly from 2.48 ns to 2.03 ns, indicating that Cu2+ can dynamically quench the FS fluorescence to a certain extent, but the very strong fluorescence intensity of FS resulted in this quenching process not being significant. It is noteworthy that the fluorescence lifetime of UIO-66@FS composite solution was 1.78 ns before detection of Cu2+ and 0.61 ns after detection, and this significant change tentatively proves that the dynamic quenching mechanism involved in Cu2+ is one of the factors to quench the fluorescence intensity of UIO-66@FS [54].

In addition, the composites were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) absorption spectroscopy as well as powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) with ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectroscopy, and the detection mechanism was analyzed at the structural level. Firstly, FTIR test analysis was carried out, as shown in Fig. S8, the FTIR pattern clearly demonstrated that the absorption peak of CO belonging to the carboxylic acid ligand of UIO-66 was obviously weakened after the detection of Cu2+ in the composites, and the probable reason was due to the coordination of Cu2+ with the carboxylic acid ligand of UIO-66. Second, as shown in Fig. S9, the UV absorption spectra clearly demonstrated that the intensity of the UV absorption peaks belonging to the carboxylic acid functional group near 240 nm was enhanced before and after the detection of Cu2+ for both UIO-66 and UIO-66@FS, which further proved that Cu2+ was coordinated with the carboxylic acid ligand. In general, the coordination of competing metal ions with metal–organic frameworks usually leads to the decomposition of the MOF framework, which will be clearly presented in the XRD patterns. Therefore, the crystal structure of the composites was analyzed by XRD technique, as shown in Fig. S10, from which it can be clearly seen that the crystal structure of UIO-66 was disrupted after the detection of Cu2+, and the strongest diffraction peak with a diffraction angle of 7.35° was significantly weakened and the rest of the diffraction peaks changed significantly after the detection of Cu2+.The green fluorescence property of UIO-66@FS essentially originated from the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from the UIO-66 ligand to the FS molecule, but due to the trapping of Cu2+ by UIO-66@FS caused the Cu2+ entering the interior of the composite to coordinate with the carboxylic acid ligand, which interfered with the electronic structure of the ligand, which disordered the fluorescence resonance energy transfer process from the UIO-66 ligand to the FS molecule, leading to a significant decrease in the energy transfer efficiency, thus quenching the green fluorescence of the composites [55,56]. In addition, as shown in Fig. S11A and B, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images more visually reflected that the octahedral morphology of the composites was significantly disrupted in the presence of Cu2+, but presented a nanosheet structure, which might be the morphology of the complex formed by the coordination of Cu2+ with the carboxylic acid ligand [57], and the corresponding EDS elemental analysis also confirmed the presence of various elements.

In conclusion, we rationally designed an excellent sensing material for the detection of Cu2+ in water. UIO-66@FS has the advantages of simple synthesis, and it can still maintain good fluorescence performance in complex environments, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 62.03 %. In the fluorescence quenching detection of Cu2+, UIO-66@FS has the advantages of fast reaction speed, wide linear range (2.5 µM-500 µM), low detection limit (0.246 µM), and visualization under UV light. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching originates from dynamic quenching and the coordination of Cu2+ with the carboxylic acid ligand in UIO-66@FS, which disrupts the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from the UIO-66 ligand to the FS molecule, leading to a decrease in energy and then quenching the fluorescence emission. The sensor was also utilized for Cu2+ detection in real environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries. This sensing platform has full research potential for Cu2+ detection in real environmental water samples.
